document.critique: Tested all machine counts, eligibility, same/different constraints, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 1 fails J12=X with J10=Z; candidates 0, 2, 3, and 4 survive. Recalculated costs as 158, 143, 150, and 138. Candidate 4 is uniquely cheapest, so retain the preceding proposal.

Tested capacities, all pairwise constraints, implications, and exact-one rules. Candidate 0 survives with cost 142. Candidates 1 and 4 fail the J7=X implication; candidate 2 fails the exact-one X rule; candidate 3 survives but costs 143. Thus retain the preceding assignment and correct its total cost to 142.

Rechecked capacities, all pair restrictions, domain limits, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 0 satisfies every constraint and costs 146. Candidates 1 and 2 are feasible but cost 156 and 149; candidate 3 fails the exact-one-X rule. The preceding proposal survives and remains optimal.

Tested all machine counts, pairwise constraints, implications, exact-one rules, and recalculated costs. Candidates 1 and 2 fail respectively on J1=J11=Z and J6=W with J3=Y. Candidates 0, 3, and 4 survive, costing 134, 189, and 149. Candidate 0 remains uniquely cheapest, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

I evaluated all 17 Boolean statements under the proposed assignment. Every statement’s truth value matches its speaker’s type; in particular, P’s IFF is false and L’s IFF is false, matching both liars. No candidate alternative survives all equations, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

I tested all four candidates by evaluating each nested statement against its assigned type. Candidate 1 fails at F, candidate 2 fails at C/Q, and candidate 3 fails at M. Candidate 0 survives: C’s statement is false (both OR branches are false), while M’s two IFF clauses are true and the remaining statements also match. Thus the returned answer retains the preceding proposal.

I independently evaluated all 17 nested statements under the proposed assignment. Each statement matches its speaker’s type: notably H, I, and L are false; N and P are true; and Q is false. The other candidates fail at least one speaker-consistency check. Thus candidate 3 survives and the preceding proposal should be retained.

I re-evaluated all five candidates directly. Candidate 0 fails at C, candidate 1 at A, candidate 2 at J, candidate 3 at B, and candidate 4 at A. Thus no candidate fully survives; the returned mapping is the closest proposed alternative, but it cannot validly replace the preceding proposal as an exact solution.

I rechecked the phase-0 differences and found M=103, p0=2, q0=-1, t=-2, r=4, s=-2. However, phase 3 yields incompatible simultaneous equations: -54p3-3q3=43 and 45p3-55q3=38 mod 103, with no allowed p3,q3. Thus the system remains inconsistent; candidate 2 is retained only as the preceding proposal, not as a verified continuation.

I rechecked the recurrence constraints across all three candidate moduli, periodic coefficient classes, and allowed t, r, and s ranges against the supplied sequence. Candidates 0, 1, and 2 fail the modular equations or remainder requirements. The system yielding [95, 40, 45] survives the consistency checks, so I retain the preceding proposal.

I independently rechecked the recurrence at the repeated residue classes and propagated the surviving coefficient/modulus constraints through the next three indices. Candidate 1 fails the modular equations, and candidate 2 fails the first continuation check. Candidate 0 remains consistent with the verified system, so I retain the preceding proposal.

I rechecked the indexing, phase n mod 4, and modular remainder rule. The retained modulo-101 recurrence reproduces all 17 supplied terms and yields 22, then 54, then −108 ≡ 94 mod 101. The other candidate triples do not match this verified continuation, so the preceding proposal should be retained.
